← Home

Features

SWE-Squad is a fully automated incident response platform that combines intelligent model routing, iterative fix loops, semantic memory, and a rigorous stability gate to resolve production issues faster than manual workflows. Explore each capability below.

🔍

Automated Detection

SWE-Squad continuously monitors your production environment through real-time log ingestion and metric collection from every service in your stack.

Its pattern detection engine matches incoming signals against a growing library of anomaly signatures, identifying issues often before they trigger user-facing impact.

When a threshold breach or novel pattern is detected, the system generates a structured alert with full context, including service metadata, severity classification, and related historical incidents.

These alerts flow directly into the triage queue, eliminating manual toil and ensuring no issue goes unnoticed regardless of time of day.

flowchart LR
    LogSources[Log Sources] --> PD[Pattern Detection]
    PD --> AG[Alert Generation]
    AG --> TQ[Triage Queue]
🧠

Smart Model Routing

Not every incident warrants the same level of AI horsepower. SWE-Squad uses a complexity analyzer to classify each incoming issue and route it to the most cost-effective model capable of handling it.

Fast triage and classification tasks are handled by lightweight Haiku-class models, keeping latency low and cost per incident minimal. Investigation and root-cause analysis are delegated to mid-tier Sonnet-class models that balance depth with speed.

Only the most complex, multi-service failures are escalated to Opus-class models, ensuring that expensive capacity is reserved for the problems that truly require it.

This tiered approach typically reduces model spend by 60-80% compared to routing every incident through the most capable model.

flowchart TD
    Incident[Incoming Incident] --> CA[Complexity Analyzer]
    CA -->|Simple| Haiku[Haiku - Triage & Classification]
    CA -->|Moderate| Sonnet[Sonnet - Investigation & RCA]
    CA -->|Complex| Opus[Opus - Deep Multi-Service Fixes]
🔄

Keep/Discard Fix Loop

When a proposed fix fails validation, SWE-Squad does not simply retry blindly. Instead, it enters a structured iterative loop where the agent reviews the specific failure reasons, adjusts its approach, and generates an improved patch.

Each retry carries forward the full failure context from previous attempts, so the agent never repeats the same mistake twice. The loop is bounded by a maximum of three attempts to prevent infinite cycles.

If all three attempts are exhausted without a passing result, the system escalates the incident to a human engineer along with the complete diagnostic trace, including every attempted fix and its failure reasons.

flowchart TD
    GF[Generate Fix] --> V[Validate]
    V -->|Pass| D[Deploy]
    V -->|Fail| RF[Review Failures]
    RF --> AA[Adjust Approach]
    AA -->|Attempt <= 3| GF
    AA -->|Attempt > 3| EH[Escalate to Human]
🛡️

Ralph Wiggum Stability Gate

No fix ships unless it passes every single check. Named after the principle of uncompromising gatekeeping, the Ralph Wiggum Stability Gate is an eight-stage validation pipeline that catches regressions, security vulnerabilities, and subtle bugs before they reach production.

The pipeline runs syntax checks, unit tests, linting, type checking, security scanning, regression testing, scope verification, and a confidence score assessment in sequence. A failure at any stage stops the entire pipeline and feeds the failure reason back into the fix loop.

Only fixes that clear all eight gates are approved for deployment, giving teams confidence that automated patches meet the same quality bar as human-reviewed code.

flowchart LR
    Fix[Proposed Fix] --> S[Syntax Check]
    S --> UT[Unit Tests]
    UT --> L[Lint]
    L --> TC[Type Check]
    TC --> SS[Security Scan]
    SS --> RT[Regression Test]
    RT --> SC[Scope Check]
    SC --> CS[Confidence Score]
    CS -->|All Pass| Deploy[Deploy]
🧠

Semantic Memory

SWE-Squad maintains an accumulated knowledge store that grows smarter with every incident it resolves. When a new issue arrives, the system first matches it against this semantic memory to check whether a similar problem has been seen before.

If a match is found, the cached fix and its context are retrieved instantly, often resolving the issue without invoking a model at all. This means previously encountered error patterns are handled at zero model cost and near-zero latency.

When no match exists, the agent investigates the issue from scratch and then stores its findings back into the memory for future reuse. Over time, the system becomes progressively faster and more accurate as its knowledge base expands.

flowchart TD
    II[Incoming Issue] --> MAM[Match Against Memory]
    MAM -->|Hit| RCF[Return Cached Fix]
    MAM -->|Miss| INV[Investigate]
    INV --> SNF[Store New Finding]
    SNF --> RCF

Trajectory Distillation

Every time SWE-Squad resolves an incident, it produces a detailed trajectory of the steps taken: exploration paths, code changes, test results, and the reasoning behind each decision. Trajectory distillation compresses these successful trajectories into concise, reusable playbooks.

These distilled playbooks capture the essential steps and context needed to resolve a class of similar issues, stripping away the exploratory dead ends and intermediate failures. When a future incident matches a playbook pattern, the system can skip directly to the proven solution path.

This dramatically reduces both resolution time and token cost for recurring issue categories, compounding the system
